items.question: If I ___ the lottery, I'd travel the world.
explanations: 'Win' (present simple) is for the first conditional, a real future.
Correct! An imaginary situation takes the past simple in the if-clause: 'If I won'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
The if-clause never uses 'would' — that goes in the result.

items.question: If I had more time, I ___ learn the piano.
explanations: 'Will' belongs to the first conditional (a real future).
Correct! The result of a second conditional uses 'would': 'I would learn'.
'Would have' belongs to the third conditional (an imagined past).
'Can' is a real-future modal, not the imaginary result here.

items.question: If I ___ rich, I'd buy an island.
explanations: The if-clause doesn't use 'would'.
Correct! In the second conditional, use 'were' for all subjects: 'If I were rich'.
'Am' is present simple, but this is an imaginary situation.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.

items.question: If I ___ you, I'd apologise.
explanations: 'Was' is common in speech, but standard English uses 'were' here.
Correct! The advice form is 'If I were you'.
'Am' is present simple, but this is hypothetical.
The if-clause doesn't use 'would'.

items.question: If she lived closer, she ___ visit more often.
explanations: 'Will' belongs to the first conditional.
Correct! The imaginary result uses 'would': 'she would visit'.
'Would have' belongs to the third conditional.
'Can' doesn't fit the imaginary result here.

items.question: If we ___ a bigger house, we'd have a garden.
explanations: 'Have' (present) is for real situations; this is imaginary.
Correct! The if-clause uses the past simple: 'If we had a bigger house'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: Correct this: 'If I would have a car, I'd drive to work.'
options: If I had a car, I'd drive to work.
If I have a car, I'd drive to work.
If I would had a car, I'd drive to work.
If I will have a car, I'd drive to work.
explanations: Correct! The if-clause of a second conditional uses the past simple: 'If I had a car'.
'If I have' is present simple — that's the first conditional.
'Would had' is grammatically wrong.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.

items.question: If I had wings, I ___ fly everywhere.
explanations: 'Will' belongs to the first conditional.
Correct! 'Could' can replace 'would' in the result: 'I could fly'.
'Would have' belongs to the third conditional.
'Can' is a real-future modal, not this imaginary result.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I don't have money, so I can't travel.'
options: If I had money, I'd travel.
If I have money, I'll travel.
If I had had money, I'd have travelled.
I travel if I have money.
explanations: Correct! An unreal present situation takes the second conditional: 'If I had money, I'd travel'.
'If I have… I'll travel' is a real future (first conditional), not this imaginary case.
'Had had… I'd have travelled' is the third conditional, about the past.
'I travel if I have money' is a zero conditional (a habit).

items.question: It's very unlikely, but if I ___ president, I'd change the law.
explanations: 'Am' is present simple, but this is an unlikely, imaginary situation.
Correct! An unlikely situation takes 'were' for all subjects: 'if I were president'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.
